Spline Model Comparison: Linear vs Natural Splines vs Penalized Splines

Variance Partition Development

2026-04-27

Overview

This document explores the differences between three modeling strategies for longitudinal RNA-seq data: 1. Linear Mixed Models (lmer with linear terms): Assumes a strictly linear relationship. 2. Unpenalized Natural Splines (lmer with ns(df=3)): Allows for non-linearity but uses a fixed degree of flexibility, risking overfitting on purely linear data. 3. Penalized Smoothing Splines (gamm4 with s(bs='cr')): Automatically tunes the degrees of freedom to prevent overfitting, but is computationally intensive.

We will simulate 6 genes with different biological trajectories, fit all three models, compare the fitted curves, extract hypothesis testing p-values, and compare variance partitioning results.

library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
library(gamm4)
library(splines)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(patchwork)
library(knitr)

set.seed(42)

1. Simulate Data

We simulate 30 subjects sampled over 8 timepoints. We generate 6 distinct gene expression profiles to stress-test the models: * Gene 1 (Linear): A strict straight-line relationship. * Gene 2 (One Bend): A U-shape or inverted U-shape. * Gene 3 (Complex Wave): A sine wave (highly non-linear). * Gene 4 (Flat/Null): No relationship with age, pure noise. * Gene 5 (Sharp Step): A sudden change in expression at age 40. * Gene 6 (High Frequency): Rapid fluctuations that exceed 3 degrees of freedom.

n_subj <- 30
n_timepoints <- 8

subjects <- data.frame(
  Subject = factor(paste0("S", sprintf("%02d", 1:n_subj))),
  Start_Age = runif(n_subj, 20, 60),
  Subj_Eff = rnorm(n_subj, 0, 2)
)

df <- expand.grid(Subject = subjects$Subject, Timepoint = 1:n_timepoints)
df <- left_join(df, subjects, by = "Subject")
df$Age <- df$Start_Age + (df$Timepoint * 2) + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 0.5)

# Gene 1: Strictly Linear
df$Gene1_Linear <- 0.5 * df$Age + df$Subj_Eff + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 3)

# Gene 2: One Bend (Inverted U-shape)
df$Gene2_OneBend <- -0.02 * (df$Age - 50)^2 + df$Subj_Eff + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 3)

# Gene 3: Highly Non-linear (Sine wave)
df$Gene3_Complex <- 10 * sin(df$Age / 5) + df$Subj_Eff + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 3)

# Gene 4: Flat (Null - no age effect)
df$Gene4_Flat <- 0 * df$Age + df$Subj_Eff + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 3)

# Gene 5: Sharp Step (e.g. sudden drop at age 40)
df$Gene5_Step <- ifelse(df$Age > 40, -5, 5) + df$Subj_Eff + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 3)

# Gene 6: High Frequency (Multiple bends, underfit by df=3)
df$Gene6_HighFreq <- 8 * sin(df$Age / 3) + 4 * cos(df$Age / 2) + df$Subj_Eff + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 3)

df_long <- pivot_longer(df, cols = starts_with("Gene"), names_to = "Gene", values_to = "Expression")

2. Fit Models and Predict

genes <- c("Gene1_Linear", "Gene2_OneBend", "Gene3_Complex", "Gene4_Flat", "Gene5_Step", "Gene6_HighFreq")
test_results <- list()
plot_data <- list()
models_cache <- list() # Save models for variance partitioning

# Create a dense grid of Age for smooth prediction lines (ignoring Subject variation)
grid <- data.frame(Age = seq(min(df$Age), max(df$Age), length.out=200))

for (g in genes) {
  # Formulas
  f_null <- as.formula(paste(g, "~ 1 + (1|Subject)"))
  f_lin  <- as.formula(paste(g, "~ Age + (1|Subject)"))
  f_ns   <- as.formula(paste(g, "~ ns(Age, df=3) + (1|Subject)"))
  f_gam  <- as.formula(paste(g, "~ s(Age, bs='cr')"))
  
  # Fits (Using REML=FALSE for valid Likelihood Ratio Tests of fixed effects)
  fit_null <- lmer(f_null, data=df, REML=FALSE)
  fit_lin  <- lmer(f_lin, data=df, REML=FALSE)
  fit_ns   <- lmer(f_ns, data=df, REML=FALSE)
  fit_gam  <- gamm4(f_gam, random = ~(1|Subject), data=df, REML=FALSE)
  
  models_cache[[g]] <- list(lin = fit_lin, ns = fit_ns, gam = fit_gam)
  
  # Hypothesis Tests
  pval_lin <- anova(fit_null, fit_lin)$`Pr(>Chisq)`[2]
  pval_ns <- anova(fit_null, fit_ns)$`Pr(>Chisq)`[2]
  pval_gam <- summary(fit_gam$gam)$s.table["s(Age)", "p-value"]
  
  test_results[[g]] <- data.frame(
    Gene = g,
    Pval_Linear = pval_lin,
    Pval_NS_df3 = pval_ns,
    Pval_GAM = pval_gam
  )
  
  # Predictions for plotting (marginalizing over Subject, setting random effect to 0)
  grid$Pred_Linear <- predict(fit_lin, newdata=grid, re.form=NA)
  grid$Pred_NS_df3 <- predict(fit_ns, newdata=grid, re.form=NA)
  grid$Pred_GAM <- predict(fit_gam$gam, newdata=grid)
  
  grid_long <- pivot_longer(grid, cols=starts_with("Pred"), names_to="Model", values_to="Prediction")
  grid_long$Model <- gsub("Pred_", "", grid_long$Model)
  grid_long$Gene <- g
  
  plot_data[[g]] <- grid_long
}

df_results <- bind_rows(test_results)
df_plot <- bind_rows(plot_data)

3. Visual Comparison of Fitted Curves

ggplot() +
  # Raw data
  geom_point(data = df_long, aes(x = Age, y = Expression, color = Subject), alpha = 0.2) +
  # Fitted lines
  geom_line(data = df_plot, aes(x = Age, y = Prediction, linetype = Model), linewidth = 1.2, color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~Gene, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(title = "Model Fits: Linear vs Natural Spline vs GAMM",
       subtitle = "Black lines represent the population-level predictions (marginalizing over Subject)",
       y = "Expression") +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Linear" = "dotted", "NS_df3" = "dashed", "GAM" = "solid"))

Observations from Plots:

  1. Gene 1 (Linear): Both NS_df3 and GAM perfectly recover the linear relationship without noticeably overfitting the noise.
  2. Gene 2 (One Bend): NS_df3 and GAM both capture the quadratic peak beautifully. The linear model completely fails.
  3. Gene 3 (Complex): The GAM captures the full sine wave. NS_df3 struggles because it is constrained to only 3 degrees of freedom.
  4. Gene 4 (Flat/Null): The GAM shrinks perfectly to a flat line. The NS_df3 shows slight wiggles as it attempts to fit noise, illustrating the mild overfitting risk.
  5. Gene 5 (Sharp Step): Both spline methods struggle with pure discontinuities. NS_df3 tends to “smooth out” the sharp jump significantly, whereas GAMM adapts a bit better by using more degrees of freedom but still can’t form a perfect step function.
  6. Gene 6 (High Frequency): NS_df3 severely underfits this rapid oscillation because 3 degrees of freedom simply cannot capture that many bends. GAMM captures it successfully.

4. Hypothesis Testing Comparison

kable(df_results, digits = 5, format.args = list(scientific = TRUE), 
      caption = "P-values: Significance of Age Effect")
P-values: Significance of Age Effect
Gene Pval_Linear Pval_NS_df3 Pval_GAM
Gene1_Linear 0.0000e+00 0.0000e+00 0.0000e+00
Gene2_OneBend 2.4000e-04 0.0000e+00 0.0000e+00
Gene3_Complex 7.2100e-03 0.0000e+00 0.0000e+00
Gene4_Flat 6.4447e-01 9.3929e-01 6.4056e-01
Gene5_Step 0.0000e+00 0.0000e+00 0.0000e+00
Gene6_HighFreq 8.6154e-01 0.0000e+00 0.0000e+00

Statistical Observations:

  • The likelihood ratio test correctly protects the NS_df3 from false positives on Gene 4 despite visual wiggles.
  • NS_df3 detects the true effect robustly even when it partially underfits (Gene 3, Gene 5, Gene 6), though with slightly less power than GAMM in cases where 3 df is a poor fit.

5. Variance Partitioning Comparison

Here we implement the “Realized Predictions” variance partitioning approach for all three models to see how much variance they attribute to Age vs Subject vs Residuals. This allows us to see how the mathematical constraints of each model impact the final variance fractions.

calc_vp <- function(model, model_type = "lmer") {
  if (model_type == "lmer") {
    # Extract fixed effects variance
    X <- getME(model, "X")
    beta <- fixef(model)
    # Remove intercept
    non_int <- colnames(X) != "(Intercept)"
    X_fixed <- X[, non_int, drop=FALSE]
    beta_fixed <- beta[non_int]
    
    # Calculate realized prediction variance for fixed effects (Age)
    pred_fixed <- as.numeric(X_fixed %*% beta_fixed)
    var_age <- var(pred_fixed)
    if (is.na(var_age)) var_age <- 0
    
    # Extract random effects variance (Subject)
    Z <- getME(model, "Z")
    b <- getME(model, "b")
    pred_subj <- as.numeric(Z %*% b)
    var_subj <- var(pred_subj)
    
    var_resid <- sigma(model)^2
    
    total <- var_age + var_subj + var_resid
    return(data.frame(Age = var_age/total*100, Subject = var_subj/total*100, Residual = var_resid/total*100))
    
  } else if (model_type == "gamm") {
    mer_obj <- model$mer
    # Fixed effects (linear part of spline)
    X <- getME(mer_obj, "X")
    beta <- fixef(mer_obj)
    
    spline_fixed_name <- "Xs(Age)Fx1"
    
    if (spline_fixed_name %in% colnames(X)) {
       pred_lin <- as.numeric(X[, spline_fixed_name, drop=FALSE] %*% beta[spline_fixed_name])
    } else {
       pred_lin <- rep(0, nrow(X))
    }
    
    # Random effects (Subject and non-linear spline)
    Z <- getME(mer_obj, "Z")
    b <- getME(mer_obj, "b")
    gp <- getME(mer_obj, "Gp")
    names_re <- names(ranef(mer_obj))
    
    pred_nonlin <- rep(0, nrow(Z))
    var_subj <- 0
    
    for (i in seq_along(names_re)) {
      start <- gp[i] + 1
      end <- gp[i+1]
      Z_grp <- Z[, start:end, drop = FALSE]
      b_grp <- b[start:end]
      pred_grp <- as.numeric(Z_grp %*% b_grp)
      
      if (names_re[i] == "Xr") {
        pred_nonlin <- pred_grp
      } else if (names_re[i] == "Subject") {
        var_subj <- var(pred_grp)
      }
    }
    
    # Correct calculation: Combine linear and non-linear predictions before variance
    pred_age_total <- pred_lin + pred_nonlin
    var_age <- var(pred_age_total)
    
    var_resid <- sigma(mer_obj)^2
    total <- var_age + var_subj + var_resid
    return(data.frame(Age = var_age/total*100, Subject = var_subj/total*100, Residual = var_resid/total*100))
  }
}

vp_results <- list()
for (g in genes) {
  mods <- models_cache[[g]]
  
  vp_lin <- calc_vp(mods$lin, "lmer") %>% mutate(Model = "Linear", Gene = g)
  vp_ns <- calc_vp(mods$ns, "lmer") %>% mutate(Model = "NS_df3", Gene = g)
  vp_gam <- calc_vp(mods$gam, "gamm") %>% mutate(Model = "GAMM", Gene = g)
  
  vp_results[[g]] <- bind_rows(vp_lin, vp_ns, vp_gam)
}

df_vp <- bind_rows(vp_results) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = c("Age", "Subject", "Residual"), names_to = "Component", values_to = "Pct_Explained")

# Plot Variance Partitioning
ggplot(df_vp, aes(x = Model, y = Pct_Explained, fill = Component)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack", color = "black", alpha = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~Gene, ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Age" = "#fc8d62", "Subject" = "#8da0cb", "Residual" = "#e78ac3")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Variance Partitioning Comparison", y = "% Variance Explained")

Variance Partitioning Observations:

  1. Linear Data (Gene 1): All three methods accurately attribute similar amounts of variance to Age.
  2. Non-Linear Data (Gene 2, 3, 5, 6): Linear severely underestimates the variance explained by Age because it cannot fit the curve. The missed variance is dumped into the Residual.
  3. GAMM vs NS_df3: Because GAMM uses more degrees of freedom, it explains slightly more variance in complex curves (Gene 3, Gene 6). NS_df3 explains less variance in these cases because it underfits, transferring the unexplained variance to Residuals.
  4. Flat Data (Gene 4): All models correctly identify that Age explains ~0% of the variance, emphasizing that standard lme4 variance partitioning with ns() successfully handles the prediction matrix without inflating the fraction of variance explained.

6. Conclusion

  • GAMM (gamm4): The most flexible and statistically complete method for partitioning variance in complex trajectories, but very slow.
  • Natural Spline (ns(df=3)): An excellent, ultra-fast alternative. While it may struggle visually on very sharp discontinuities or highly complex waves (Genes 5 and 6), it performs comparably to GAMM on standard non-linear biology (Gene 2) and calculates variance fractions robustly using standard mixed model frameworks.
